runs.interpretation: The compensated labor supply elasticity (substitution effect) for primary earners located in the 7th decile of the U.S. earnings distribution within a standard microsimulation framework.
citations: Saez, E. (2002). Optimal Income Transfer Programs: Intensive vs. Extensive Labor Supply Responses
Chetty, R. (2012). Bounds on Elasticities with Optimization Frictions
Blundell, R., & MaCurdy, T. (1999). Labor Supply: A Review of Alternative Approaches
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit lower labor supply elasticities than secondary earners. In the middle-to-upper deciles (like decile 7), there is significant attachment to the labor force, meaning substitution effects are typically small but positive. Elasticities in structural microsimulation models usually fall between 0.05 and 0.2, reflecting a consensus that while marginal tax changes matter, they rarely induce dramatic hours adjustments for primary earners at this income level.

  "interpretation": "The compensated labor supply elasticity (substitution effect) for primary earners located in the 7th decile of the U.S. earnings distribution within a standard microsimulation framework.",

  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Primary earners generally exhibit lower labor supply elasticities than secondary earners. In the middle-to-upper deciles (like decile 7), there is significant attachment to the labor force, meaning substitution effects are typically small but positive. Elasticities in structural microsimulation models usually fall between 0.05 and 0.2, reflecting a consensus that while marginal tax changes matter, they rarely induce dramatic hours adjustments for primary earners at this income level."
}

  "reasoning_summary": "Primary earners, especially in middle-to-high deciles, typically exhibit very low labor supply elasticities. }
